title: The Looping Conversation
description: Practice reflecting instead of reacting — the foundation of real listening.
steps: Partner 1 speaks for 5 minutes about something that mattered to them this week — anything at all.
Partner 2 listens without interrupting. When Partner 1 finishes, reflect back: 'What I heard you say is…'
Partner 2 checks: 'Did I get that right?'
Partner 1 clarifies anything that was missed. Then switch roles.
After both partners have spoken, share one word each about how it felt to be truly heard.
reflection: What was harder — the speaking or the listening? What does that tell you about your default style?

title: The XYZ Statement
description: Say the hard thing without triggering a defensive response.
steps: Each partner independently thinks of one thing that bothered them recently.
Write it down in the attacking form first: 'You always…' or 'You never…'
Now rewrite it using the format: 'When you do X in situation Y, I feel Z.'
Take turns reading your softened version out loud. The listener's only job is to say: 'Thank you for telling me.'
No rebuttals, no defenses — just acknowledgment. Discuss how the rewrite felt different.
reflection: Which version felt more true to what you actually needed? What made the rewrite harder to say?

title: The 30-Second Hug
description: Reconnect through sustained physical presence — simple, powerful, and underused.
steps: Stand facing each other. Set a timer for 30 seconds.
Hug — not a quick squeeze. A real hold. Let your breathing slow down.
Don't talk during the hug. Just notice what you feel.
When the timer ends, stay in the hug for 5 more seconds if it feels right.
Each share one word about how it felt.
reflection: Did 30 seconds feel long or short? What does your answer tell you about how comfortable you are with sustained closeness?

title: The Softened Startup
description: Begin a difficult conversation gently enough that your partner can actually hear you.
steps: Each partner writes down one topic you've been avoiding because it tends to blow up.
Write your opening line the way you'd usually say it — honest, probably a bit blaming.
Now rewrite the opening using: 'I feel ___ about ___ and I need ___.'
Take turns reading your softened opening out loud. The listener repeats back what they heard.
Discuss: what made the softened version easier or harder to say?
reflection: What do you protect when you start a conversation in attack mode? What does softening it risk for you?

title: Repair Attempt Practice
description: Build a toolkit of small gestures that de-escalate conflict before it becomes damage.
steps: Together, brainstorm 10 things one of you could say or do mid-argument that signals 'I don't want to fight — I want to reconnect.'
Examples to spark ideas: a specific phrase, a touch on the arm, a hand signal, a shared joke, leaving the room for 5 minutes, making tea.
Each partner picks 3 from the list that they'd genuinely respond to (not just things they'd do).
Write your chosen 3 on a card or in your phones. These are your official repair attempts.
Agree: if you use one of these during a conflict, the other person commits to recognizing it and pausing.
reflection: Which repair attempt felt most natural to offer? Which felt hardest to receive — and why?

title: Appreciation Flashback
description: Reactivate warmth by deliberately returning to what you love about each other.
steps: Each partner independently writes 3 specific things they genuinely appreciate about the other. Not general — specific memories or traits.
Take turns reading your list out loud. The listener's only job is to receive it without deflecting ('Oh, that's nothing…').
After both have read, discuss: was there anything that surprised you about what your partner noticed?
Optionally: save your lists and read them again during your next rough patch.
reflection: Why do we forget to say these things? What gets in the way of appreciation becoming a daily habit?

title: The State of the Union
description: A structured 20-minute weekly check-in that prevents small problems from becoming big ones.
steps: Set a timer. Each partner gets 10 minutes, uninterrupted.
Partner 1 answers: 'How did I feel loved this week? What was hard? What do I need more of next week?'
Partner 2 listens without responding — just takes notes if helpful.
Switch. Partner 2 answers the same three questions.
After both have spoken, take 5 minutes to agree on one specific thing each of you will do differently next week.
reflection: What made it easier or harder to be honest in this format? What would make you want to do this every week?

title: Dreams Behind the Fight
description: Discover what deeper need or value is actually driving your most persistent conflict.
steps: Pick one recurring argument topic — the one you've had 10+ times without resolving.
Each partner independently writes: 'When we fight about this, what I'm really afraid of is… What I'm really wanting is…'
Share your answers out loud — no debating, just listening.
Together, see if you can identify the shared value underneath both of your positions (e.g., you both want security — you just define it differently).
Write the shared value at the top of the topic: 'We both want ___. We just show it differently.'
reflection: Did finding the shared value change how the fight feels? What would change if you started from the shared value, not the surface disagreement?

title: The 5-Year Letter
description: Reconnect with your shared future by writing to the couple you're building toward.
steps: Each partner independently writes a letter from the future — dated 5 years from today — to their current selves.
In the letter: describe what your relationship looks and feels like. What did you build? How do you spend your days? What do you feel toward each other?
Read your letters to each other out loud.
Discuss: what's the same in both letters? What's different? What does the difference reveal?
Together, write one sentence that captures the shared future you both want.
reflection: What in your partner's letter surprised you? What in your own letter surprised you?

title: The Intimacy Map
description: Understand each other's actual intimacy needs — not the ones you assume.
steps: Each partner rates (1–5) their current satisfaction with: emotional closeness, physical affection, sexual connection, intellectual intimacy, spiritual/values alignment.
Also note: what makes you feel most desired? What makes you feel pressured?
Share your ratings and notes. The listener doesn't defend — just asks: 'What would a 5 look like for you in that area?'
Identify the area with the biggest gap between partners — this is your starting point.
Agree on one specific, low-stakes thing you'll each try this week to move that needle.
reflection: Which area was hardest to rate honestly? What made it vulnerable to admit?
